item.title: Friday Dump
item.description:
God bless you?
Whole aisle to himself, yessir
For the supremely lazy
Mmmmmmm
Roughing it
My burlesque name!? Spelled just right!? Thanks Honey!
Bored as fuck
At 3 am, mostly
The two extremes
I'll take either's lifestyle
Bitch ass brain, making me take unaffordable drugs to reach baseline
*Insert Nelson Ha Ha gif here*
Yup
No one likes a dry pussycat
New Rick Roll
Gimme gimme gimme
Oh, Miette
Confusing attention for affection again, huh?
Satisfying crunch
Cuddlemonsters unite!
He he
Feels Egyptian in style
Silly critters
One corner of the room, maybe
We literally fought a World War over this...I will end you on sight
Need room for activities
Good fuckin luck, boy-o
Womp womp
I just hear JFK saying hahd
I don't dress it, but I am in fact very much both
Libra with a Virgo cusp...guess I'm undercooked
Fucking yogurt talking shi...oh, I'm the asshole
The fingertip blood just gets me
Spot on
Because praying ain't shit
It's true!
Megan Mullally, for the win, every time
I can't stop you, Paige
WAP
Cock of the walk
Self care is important
Want some food, or lost your appetite
Grandma gives gains
That's a no from me, dog
Perpetually
Weird
She's so sweet about it
??
Growing into its head
Double wrapped

item.description:
I'm just waiting for Trump to declare a literal captain planet villain as head of the EPA.
B R I C K
we did some weird shit.
p o o p
everywhere
Gnostic Christianity is really wild, according to the Gnostics, Jesus named Simon "Peter" because he was dumb as a rock.
owo
GIRLCOCK HEADQUARTERS ARE WATCHING YOU POOP!
http://discord.abbii.gay: For Chats http://twitch.abbii.gay: For Stream http://tumblr.abbii.gay: For shitposts http://mastodon.abbii.gay: For microblogging http://bsky.abbii.gay: For Bluesky The internet is fucking weird.

item.dc:creator: spectrumchris

item.title: It's time for Funny Friday! Because the world desperately needs some laughs
item.description:
Yeah, that'd do it. Hey all, and welcome to another edition of Funny Friday, where we try to end the work week on a high note with some laughs and good humor. As always, there'll be a link to some more sexual humor in a private folder, so as to not offend the mods. So look for the link in the comments and make sure you get all of the funny.
Well, she did get his number!
Even the Yiga masks are nervous and sweating!
Sorry if this let you down.
0 for 2, Witch King.
The history of England and Ireland, abridged.
You've got to be a masochist to take it that hard.
The start of the greatest love story of the Imperium.
Sauron isn't the only one with an all-seeing eye.
It might be worth letting him drink some of my blood... (full video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Kqe3PKGcGkY)
Cruel and unusual spellcasting.
It stops pecking or it gets the pit trap again...
I haven't seen a burn this ruthless since flamethrowers were being used on Japanese bunkers.
Not a single lie detected.
There's nothing like sharing a pun (or puns) that made you facepalm.
Instead of making my dumps all nerd humor, every now and then I have to throw in a Classical Mythology reference or two. Keep the place classy.
Not everyone has time to do their makeup in the morning, okay?!
Gimli was secretly the biggest chad of LOTR?
I'm thinking knife beats hammer (both short range weapons, but knife can do damage more easily), shovel beat knife (longer range on the shovel), hammer beats shovel. (Shovel is kind of clumsy and unwieldy, a hammer is a lot more optimized for a good swing.)
You can give me that dirty look all you want, it just makes it funnier.
Yeah, but the batteries in the flashlight need to run out to add to the atmosphere!
Nothing but freaks in those sheets.
This 90s musical throwback is brought to you by your back and knee pain medications. Don't forget to take them.
Cast it into the orcs! Use it!
Everyone can have a bad day. The accordion was on point though.
Formats and venues change, but people stay the same.
Painfully accurate.
With all the features cat owners get to enjoy waking up to.
There are some things you just can't keep inside.
If he did that, I think he was looking for a way to get fired.
Once again, a cop escalates the situation...
That's the only way it was going to happen...
Does every guy have a "Tried to warn her it's been awhile but she didn't listen" story? Because I know I do...
Since I don't have a cat and my dog died last year, this is the closest you get to a cat or dog tax. Hope you had fun and have a great weekend. And remember there's a link to a few more jokes in the comments.

item.title: Adorable, but also extremely dangerous!
item.description: Seriously folks, secure your animals while the vehicle is in motion. Take it from a former animal control officer. In the event of a crash we'd much rather pick up your animal and keep them safe while you go to the hospital, it's not fun picking them up in a body bag. Info requested regarding good ways to secure your pets. You want a full chest harness that attaches at the front of the animal close to the center of gravity. For smaller dogs they have like half box deals that fully buckle in and have a tether inside to attach to your harness (search dog booster seat the AKC brand one that I've had for a long time works great for small dogs seen a few negative reviews of the same product just now when looking it up so maybe quality has declined?) For big dogs just the harness and a tether that clips into a seatbelt. Loose enough that they can adjust their sit/stand position but taught enough they cant leave one person's worth of seat or get off the seat onto the floor. There are some extra large dog booster seats, but I can't attest to their efficacy. All in all dog car seats do more to keep your dog comfortable and less likely to want to wiggle around. The number one thing is keeping your animal from flying around the cabin in a crash. For your cats, a STURDY crate that you can secure with a normal over the shoulder seatbelt is my personal recommendation.
item.dc:creator: Necridos

item.title: An ancient skeleton with some of the world's oldest (processed) gold found in Bulgaria
item.dc:creator: pilomotor

item.title: Shameless kitten video with a secret plea
item.description:
I’m gonna pull a shameless stunt and sucker you guys into reading this post by sharing a video of my foster kittens Pumpkin Pie, Lizzie, Butternut Squash, and Sweet Potato Casserole being super cute…but please keep reading…
I’m currently fostering my kittens through the Huntsville Humane Society, but the Huntsville Animal Shelter is in desperate need of kitten fosters! If you know of anyone in the area who can temporarily foster some young kittens, please reach out to them! You would only keep them long enough for them to grow to two pounds, and then they go back. It would only be for a few weeks in most cases! Please spread the word!
item.dc:creator: KittensForDays

item.dc:creator: MyVerySmarterUserName

item.title: Return of the Meme: 1184 (wendygo's)
item.description:
I have made coffee for the first time in days. This is an improvement. You have to keep going. We have to keep going. Let's get this bread.
I miss this man. And as he said elsewhere, most of magic is simply knowing something other people don't.
Except for Pippin. Pippin, if I let him, won't wake up until 7 or even 8! He ends up often waking up when I wake up these days.
I still have these days.
Man. This is why you need to get forklift certified bruh.
We all know now that Evangelicals don't actually respect and honor shit except money, themselves, and frumpy antichrists.
Be human, do crime.
I've realized that one of the reasons I'm so down is that I have nothing to look forward to. Well, except for my Scythian bow I ordered, but I have no idea when it's coming, so that's not exactly concrete.
I learned today in the book I'm reading that a piece of billion plus years old oceanic crust is exposed somewhere near Payson, AZ. Which is only like an hour and a half away.
I've decided I need to see this fragment of a long-lost ocean for myself. It's a geologic feature known as an ophalite; a section of Earth's oceanic crust and the underlying upper mantle that has been uplifted and exposed, and often emplaced onto continental crustal rocks and often record ancient collisions between continents and cratons.
Apparently, this chunk is pretty well preserved, covering a decent area, though I never knew the thing existed until today. This slab of old seabed is old as fuck, dating back to the Statherian eon of the Precambrian, back 1.73 BILLION years. I want to touch it. I want to smoke a cigar on this ancient ocean bottom. I want to do it before Christmas, weather permitting.
I also decided I need a more long term trip goal, so I'm going to aim for making a pilgrimage to visit Pando in Utah. Hopefully I can convince some friends to join me there. It would, incidentally, be cool to take a trip on my trip and do some mushrooms inside what is probably the oldest living organism in the world. (Pando is a clonal Quaking Aspen Grove that might be as old as 81,000 years, and also happens to be the world's largest tree.)
I suppose if any imgurians are down to camp in the woods you could meet me there, but I'm probably not going to take Pippin on the trip. I will bring some homemade mead. Oooh, I should make a special mead for this and call it Pando-monium. Not sure what ingredients I'd use though.
So anyway. Gotta keep going so I can do the things.
If nothing is possible, nothing is permitted.
Aaaaaaaaaaaaaaa
Mood
Alien landscape by Wayne Barlow
Pippin tax! Today's message from Pippin: "Breeeep? Mmmrrr. I'm sorry. [Unintelligible muttering]"
item.dc:creator: CodyBurkett

item.title: We need Steve Irwin back eeesh
item.dc:creator: wafflesdidit

item.title: So your country decided to burn itself down instead of voting in a Woman as president for a second time. Now you're worried for your safety and want to buy a gun.
item.description:
The world of firearms is vast and can be confusing, I will do my best to shed some light on the ins and outs of firearms ownership. TL:DR 1. Ask yourself some hard questions 2. Familiarize yourself with basic firearms knowledge 3. Try shooting somewhere. Either at a range or with a friend that owns. 4. Learn the laws for your area 5. Look into training 6. Research guns that suit your needs 7. Research ammunition that suits your needs. 8. Practice and train 9. Storage and maintenance 10. Be mindful
First of all who am I to be giving out this information? I have been a firearm owner for ten years now and I have worked professionally with them for the last three. Some of my responsibilities have included selling firearms, purchasing and appraising firearms, outfitting firearms with accessories, and basic instruction for people that have never fired a gun before. However I do not come from a family with a history of firearms use, so I do have some memory of what it is like to enter the firearms world essentially blind with no family or close friends to rely on for information. I am also a Canadian so I am not directly impacted by American politics, nor do I have firsthand experience dealing with American laws surrounding firearms. I do believe that gay people, trans people, and women deserve security and safety in their lives. But I am far from a master of all things firearm, so do take everything I say with a grain of salt. I am, of course, a fallible human being and I encourage anyone with corrections or further information to post those in the comments.
Before we go any further if you have considered getting in to firearms ownership I would like you to ask yourself three things. 1. Am I of sound mind and comfortable with the idea of owning a firearm myself? Owning a firearm carries the burden of responsibility. Am I myself going to through action or inaction cause harm to myself or the people around me? It is a simple fact, owning a firearm makes you more likely to be injured or killed by a firearm. Are you certain that you can follow the rules and bear the responsibility to prevent you or others from being harmed by your own gun? Have I done anything that would legally prevent me from owning a firearm? 2. Am I certain enough about my own environment that I am comfortable introducing firearms into it? Are the people you live with comfortable with being in a household with a firearm? Are you comfortable with the people around you knowing you have a firearm? When I first bought a firearm I went over it with my family beforehand. I told them that I was not going to cause harm, and took them through the things I would be doing to prevent myself or anyone else from causing harm with them. They were not fully sold right at the start, but they came around to it. Today they understand and are comfortable with having firearms in the household with them and I am comfortable with them knowing of and where the firearms are, but not having access to them. 3. If I am purchasing a firearm for self defense have I considered how I feel about using lethal force on another human? Any firearm is capable of causing a fatal wound. In an ideal scenario where you are at immediate risk of death or serious bodily injury to you and/or your loved ones and you employ a firearm, the assailants body goes into shock, the threat is stopped, the authorities are called, the assailant receives medical attention and lives to face the justice system. However ideal scenarios are few and far between. You should come to terms with the fact that any time you introduce a firearm into an incident you are creating a life or death scenario for yourself and everyone else involved. Ideally you should not be employing a firearm unless it is already a life or death scenario, but if it isn't you will have made it into one. Ok, that may have been a bunch of questions introduced as three questions. But you should ask yourself them all the same. Nobody but you needs to know your answers. There is no shame in wanted to protect yourself and your loved ones. And there is also no shame in admitting that you are uncomfortable or unready for the burdens and responsibilities of firearm ownership.
Now with the existential stuff out of the way this next section is going to be some very basic firearms education. In the image above you will find the basic rules for handling any firearm. The acronyms ACTS and PROVE aren't very common in America, but they are used in the Canadian firearms safety course and I find it helpful to have an itemized list. At this point you will have noticed there are redundancies in the rules of firearms handling, these are intentional. Designed to keep things safe even if one of the rules is broken. However when you get to the point where you are handling firearms at a store counter you can probably safely not examine the bore of the firearm you are handling. The gif of James from Top Gear pointing a shotgun at his own head has been going around for a while, and although observing the bore of a firearm is an important part of firearm safety you will get some very colorful language out of the counter attendant if you point a gun at yourself unprompted. Do regularly check the bores of firearms you own though, bore obstructions kill people. This and other images in this post are taken directly from the course material for the Canadian firearms safety course. If you are interested in the material in its entirety it is available in PDF form here https://publications.gc.ca/site/archivee-archived.html?url=https://publications.gc.ca/collections/collection_2015/grc-rcmp/PS99-2-2-1-2014-eng.pdf
Why am I using the Canadian Firearms Safety Course material in a post directed at Americans? I value the simple illustrations and point form presentation of data. Aside from your local laws and regulations the material has everything you need to know to get safely into firearms. I'd say it's worth reading in it's entirety, minus the Canada specific laws and regulations. The point of this is to get you to the point where you at least have an idea of what you are looking at when you walk into a gun store.
A fair bit of this section is just going to be a straight rip from the handbook. It's not Pulitzer winning reading but at least it is brief.
Sometimes when people are new to the firearms world they will use "bullet" and "cartridge" interchangeably. When you are at the point where you are looking to buy ammunition keep in mind that bullets are sold separately from loaded ammunition for people that make their own ammo. If you ask for just bullets you may be directed to those instead of loaded ammo, which isn't helpful unless you are set up for handloading.
In America you are more likely to see semi automatics that look like an ar15 than you are the one pictured here. But they still are sold of course.
Keep in mind the Canadian Firearms Safety Course is designed with a hunting perspective in mind and that these stances are suited for that. When you go shooting for the first time and if you receive training you will most likely be taught something different.
Ok, hopefully after reading that you won't be completely confused the first time you walk into a gun shop. Hopefully. The next recommendation I am going to make is for you to go out and try shooting. Many businesses with indoor ranges will allow people to walk in and try guns under direct supervision. I cannot give any specific examples, you will have to do the research yourself. Make sure when you are looking for ranges that you look at reviews for them. Read about how the staff treats their customers. The industry is unfortunately host to it's share of racists and misogynists, my city in particular had a range that was notorious for it's poor treatment of women. I always personally recommended a range that was run by women to anyone that mentioned they were looking. If you have a friend you trust that owns firearms you can ask them if they are willing to let you try their guns. Keep in mind that ammo is expensive and they'll probably have to clean their guns after your range visit. In the past there have been organizations like The Pink Pistols or Operation Blazing Sword that have offered training and advice to the LGBTQ+ community. Activity on their end had fallen off in recent years and the lists of their instructors and volunteer educators may be very out of date. It may still be worth it to try to reach out to one of those organizations if you are interested, but I would not solely rely on it. The Pink Pistols have a reddit page that has seen some relatively recent traffic, as well as Operation Blazing Sword's Facebook page has had posts up to the day of me writing this. You may also find allies on Reddits r/liberalgunowners. You may be able to ask if there are any friendly ranges in your area on there. It is worthwhile to point out that the top post as of writing is this; https://www.reddit.com/r/liberalgunowners/comments/1gmnq2b/clearly_we_are_all_in_this_sub_because_of/ The Pink Pistols https://www.pinkpistols.org Operation Blazing Sword https://www.blazingsword.org r/liberalgunowners https://www.reddit.com/r/liberalgunowners
What to try at a range? When and if you do go into a range to shoot I recommend trying at least one rifle one shotgun and one handgun. They don't have to be anything super specific but I would suggest trying an ar15. The reason being is that the standard ar15 cartridge, .223 Remington/ 5.56x45mm NATO, is one of the lightest currently available rifle cartridge that you'd use for self defense. It is widely used, widely available, and is something that was still in stock regularly throughout the Covid shortages. The light cartridge is compounded by the ar15 being designed to use what is called "free recoil," meaning the bolt never comes to a complete stop at the rear of the receiver during cycling. Making for a very light recoiling firearm. That and the general light nature of the firearm makes it suitable for people smaller stature or relatively less strong individuals. Next probably a pump-action shotgun. A pump action shotgun is going to be a more approachable option if you are on a tighter budget. However shotguns are generally going to have much more felt recoil than something like an ar15. To the point where if you aren't holding it tightly enough against your shoulder pocket you can leave your range day with a bruised shoulder. Semi-automatic shotguns can alleviate some of that recoil just by nature of the action taking some of the force out. At the cost of the guns costing more and potentially being more prone to user induced failures. Lastly probably a handgun. Handgun shooting for most people is significantly more difficult than shooting long guns. I've had more than a few people that have never shot before come to me under the impression that handguns are going to be significantly easier and lighter than rifles. Most of these people leave with that preconception flipped 180 degrees. Going from a long gun with three points of contact to handguns that have essentially one point of contact is a very large jump in difficulty. For comparison when I start people out on rifles I put the target ten meters away and most people can keep a shot grouping within eight to ten inches. With handguns I've seen people miss the target completely at five meters. The targets we use are 18"x28". That being said if you want something that you can keep on your person it's likely going to be a handgun. It's difficult to conceal a rifle and I can't recommend open carry. Start out by trying something in 9mm. 9x19mm Parabellum (also called 9mm Luger or 9mm NATO in NATO loadings) is the most commonly available handgun caliber in North America Training on it will be relatively inexpensive and it is light enough that most people can handle it. Something else to ask for while you try handguns is to try a "full size" and a "compact" handgun. "Full size" or sometimes "duty" handguns are the kind of thing you would see a member of the armed forces or a police officer carry. "Compact" handguns are going to be smaller and more suitable for inside the waistband or pocket carry. "Compact" handguns are generally going to be the more difficult of the two to handle, by nature of their smaller size in the same calibers. One last thing I can suggest if something like the ar15 is too much for you to handle you can look into trying a "pistol caliber carbine." Which is a rifle designed to use ammunition meant for handguns, which are lighter and less powerful than rifle calibers. The guns themselves can be very similar in weight and size to an ar15, but with much less powerful cartridges it makes them easier to handle. Any ar15 Pump Action Shotgun Full Size and Compact handgun Pistol Caliber Carbine You can say these to just about anyone working on the counter at a gun shop and they'll know what you mean.
So you have asked yourself some hard questions, familiarized yourself with basic firearm safety and terminology, and tried shooting for the first time. If you haven't already at this point you should familiarize yourself with state and local laws for your area. Individual states have a lot of leeway with how they regulate firearms within their borders. California in particular is more restrictive in some ways than Canada is about firearms ownership. I'm not going to go into much for specific details here as the United States is quite large and varied. I will at least recommend looking up laws for concealed carry in your state if you are planning on keeping a firearm on your person. The Wikipedia page for Concealed Carry in the United States does have a good basic chart for what is allowed from State to state. Be aware that crossing state lines while carrying a firearm have their own rules. And certain firearms or firearm types may not be allowed in some states. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concealed_carry_in_the_United_States The "Firearm legal topics of the United States" section of Wikipedia has a lot of vetted and cited information if you are looking for a good springboard for further research.
At this point you can consider taking a self defense class, before you've purchased your first gun. Some training courses do offer to loan firearms for the duration. I personally value doing training on the firearm you are planning on using, as you can receive tips and instruction specific to your personal firearm. But if you would like to be absolutely certain this is something you can do before you buy there is value in taking a basic handling and safety course beforehand. Courses are going to be specific to your area and depending on where you live you may need to travel to receive training. So be aware that travel and accommodation costs may be a factor on top of the course fees, any rental fees, and ammunition costs. As for what to take start by looking courses listed as "Basic Handgun" or "Basic Shotgun" depending on what you think you'll use. At any rate before or after purchasing a gun I would strongly recommend receiving training. Especially if you are planning on using a holster. One of the most common firearm injuries is caused by having a negligent discharge while holstered. Either pulling the trigger before you clear the holster or having your finger on the trigger while drawing and having the firearm get stuck and be pulled down into your trigger finger setting off the gun. You very much have to do deliberate training and practice to keep your trigger finger off the trigger and out of the trigger guard until you have the gun up and your sights on target. Bear in mind it is difficult to think and act while under duress, which a self defense situation will certainly be, training can help alleviate a persons tendency to freeze in an emergency.
What will you need while buying a gun? This will vary from state to state, in example California requires individuals to have a "Firearm Safety Certificate" to purchase a firearm. Which itself requires a brief course to obtain. But in general you will require: To be an American Citizen or lawful permanent resident. To be over the age of 18 for long guns or over the age of 21 for handguns, with some exceptions. Valid state issued photo ID. To fill out a Form 4473, which will allow your identity to be run through the National Instant Background Check System, which you will be required to pass. There may be other state specific checks involved. To bring money, firearms and ammo are expensive.
This part is going to be some personal recommendations for first firearms to purchase from myself. The guns I am going to recommend are going to be what I consider the most basic or lowest common denominator for a reliable firearm from a reputable company. There are of course many more firearms on the market and many of them are suitable for first time owners. I would like to present this as a starting point more than an end all be all list. Though I would personally consider these firearms for a self defense purchase that doesn't mean they will suit your needs or fit you in a way that you can use them comfortably and with confidence. There is no end to the amount of lists of firearms for first time buyers online and I would greatly encourage you to seek opinions outside of my own. I am confident in these suggestions as a baseline and when you are doing research into the firearms on your own I would recommend comparing what you hear about these firearms in reviews or testimonials from users to what you hear about other firearms you may be considering. The firearms scene on Youtube has gotten better over the years about admitting when a review has been paid for or if the firearm has been provided for free for review, but not everyone will admit those things. Be careful of paid reviews that are basically just advertisements. There is a competition shooting group in Canada that does 10,000 round tests of semiautomatic rifles and posts reviews and findings about them. To give you a sense of scale between a review that goes over initial impressions compared to review from someone that has done heavy use over the life of a firearm. Another recommendation I have is to try to physically hold a firearm before you buy it. You can buy anything you want online these days but especially for new owners I would consider going to a store where you can feel and inspect the firearm before purchasing. This may cost more than the prices you see online however.
Ruger AR-556, 5.56x45mm NATO/ .223 Remington I know civilian ownership of AR15 based rifles is a contentious issue. But the design has existed since 1956, the 5.56 guns have been in US military service since 1964. Any issues this design had have been fixed decades ago. The Ruger has a fixed front sight and a flip up rear sight from the factory, making it ready to use out of the box unlike a lot of other commercial offerings. It should work with any ar15 pattern magazine which are pretty much universally available everywhere coming from multiple brands. Ruger is a well known and respected major brand, they have one of the best warranties/ repair programs available for their firearms that is valid for the life of the firearm. Guides for repair and maintenance of ar15 platform rifles are plentiful and the guns are remarkably easy to disassemble for routine maintenance. Being one of the most ubiquitous firearm platforms in the United States means there is a large aftermarket for furniture, accessories, and peripherals. Stocks, grips, and handguards can be easily swapped out for user preferences. A drawback is that since it does have the fixed front sight if you do want to run an optic you have to deal with that being in your sight picture, however they do make flat top models if you are convinced you need an optic. Also being a "direct impingement" system the gun will get more dirty when firing than other designs. So be prepared for cleaning more frequently and more thoroughly when you do. You never know when 30-40 feral white hoods will end up on your lawn. About $820 retail. https://ruger.com/products/ar556/specSheets/8500.html Honorable mention for something comparable is going to be the Smith and Wesson M&P 15 line of guns. Similar to the Ruger lineup, can be a little more money depending on where you shop and what model you get.
Mossberg Maverick 88 Security, 12 Gauge 3" Don't worry, I looked up the origin of the name and it's because it was originally released in 1988. Hopefully not because of that other reason to use "88," but I can't give you a 100% guarantee. I know guys that carry these things while fishing for bear defense. These things get tossed into the back of trucks uncased, dropped in rivers, used as a boat anchor, and still function. It is the only gun in it's price range that I have no problems suggesting. Maintenance is relatively easy, the only thing you have to really worry about is stripping the screw that attaches the barrel to the action. There is an aftermarket for furniture if you end up interested in that sort of thing, Mossberg 500 accessories should be reasonably compatible. Mossberg produces accessory barrels on the off chance you want a gun that you can swap to have a rifled barrel for sabot slugs. If you're interested in hunting. 12 Gauge isn't the gentlest round to shoot but it will most certainly work in a defensive situation. I am going to recommend Federal Premium 00 Buckshot with their Flitecontrol Wad. Which will keep all of your pellets on target up to 35 yards. About $250 retail. https://www.mossberg.com/maverick-88-security-31023.html Honorable mention is going to be the Winchester SXP line of shotguns. It's probably the next step up that I would recommend. Similarly easy to use and maintain, but do expect to spend around $70 more than you would on the Maverick.
Glock 17 Gen5, 9x19mm Parabellum Standard police issue all over the world. And some military issue. Simple, reliable, polymer frame. No external safety so that is a consideration if you are going to be using it. The magazines are some of the most common and reliable out there. There are good reliable aftermarket magazines and some not so good or reliable aftermarket magazines. Glock and Magpul magazines are a good place to start. Many of the pistol caliber carbines on the market are set up to use Glock mags, or can be modified to take Glock mags. So if you want a pistol and a long gun with compatible magazines they are a good option to pair together. 30 round magazines exist if you are in a state that allows you to own them. Which isn't so much of a consideration for the pistol so much as it adds value to the compatible carbines. Hell, you can get 50 or 100 round drums if you just want something to play with on the range. Not 100% parts compatible with earlier generation Glock 17 pistols, so if you are looking for aftermarket parts make sure you check for compatibility. Doesn't have finger grooves like the earlier generations, which is nice if you have hands that don't fit finger grooves like mine. About $540 retail. https://us.glock.com/en/pistols/g17-gen5-fs-us Honorable mention is going to be a couple of things. The Smith and Wesson M&P 9 series is comparable but magazine compatibility is more limited between the handguns and carbines. I'm a grown man with tiny woman hands and the Beretta APX fits me quite well. A few of the guys I know have wives and daughters that it fits quite well. Walther PDP if a larger grip would suit you better.
Glock 48, 9x19mm Parabellum The small Glock. Slimmer grip and slimmer slide to make it more appropriate for concealed carry. Doesn't use the same magazines as the full sized Glock, so capacity is lower and there isn't anything I am aware of with magazine compatibility. Of course more difficult to use and more susceptible to user induced failures. Aside from that you still get the Glock reliability (barring user error) and reputation. About $448 retail. https://us.glock.com/en/pistols/g48-us No honorable mention here. Canada doesn't allow handguns to be carried outside of very specific scenarios so there isn't as much of a market for compact handguns. Which just means I have handled fewer compact pistols personally. Oh and Gaston Glock is big into equestrianism so you can buy Glock branded horse semen. If you'd like your horse to match your gun.
A couple more honorable mentions in the Ruger PC9, 9x19mm, and literally anything in .22 Long Rifle. The Ruger PC9 as it is a remarkably durable pistol caliber carbine, I have personally seem the bolt split in half and have no impact on the function of the gun. And it has the option to swap out the magazine well to use different handgun magazines, Glock 17 magazines included. Sold a pair to a couple of guys and they enjoyed them so much they came back and bought two more so they could keep shooting with them while the first set cooled down. Any gun chambered for the .22 long rifle cartridge because while it's not powerful enough that I would suggest it for personal defense the fundamentals of marksmanship are the same regardless of what caliber or platform you are using. .22 long rifle is light and inexpensive enough to let you get good practice under your belt without bleeding your wallet dry or wearing out your shoulder. The Ruger 10/22 rifle is one of the most common rifles in North America with a massive aftermarket. I have never had qualms with recommending them to people and they are so popular I hardly ever had to with people just coming in to pick them up. But any gun in .22lr will likely serve so buy according to your own research and tastes. .22lr is still very much capable of causing a fatal wound so you do need to take all the same care that you would with any other firearm, and though I don't recommend it for personal defense it can used if there is no other option.
Once you have your guns practice with them. Practice and train. Train on your own, train in groups, seek training, record yourself training so you can identify issues you are having. Do dry fire training. It doesn't matter what resources you have if you don't know how to employ them properly. You don't need to be John Wick but if you aren't familiar with what you are doing you may end up being a bigger threat to yourself and others than the thing you are trying to prevent. Unrelated note Keanu seemingly sweeping the cameraperson with the barrel of that gun always bothers me about that gif. Remember the fundamental rules of safe handling.
A quick note about the term "Stopping Power" that you will encounter while doing research into ammunition to use in defensive scenarios. The usual question is "why "stopping power" and not "killing power?"" The term "stopping power" isn't an attempt to use a politically correct term, or to whitewash the fact that firearms are killing tools. The nuance exists because it is possible to cause a fatal wound but not stop a perpetrator or animal from being a threat and causing bodily harm or death. An immediate death will stop someone from being a threat but an eventual death may not, both are still fatal.
What kind of ammunition should you use? The gun community at large will crucify me if I do not mention that shot placement is the most important factor in a successful use of a firearm. It doesn't matter if you have the meekest gallery gun in .22 short or the mightiest Anzio Ironworks rifle in 20mm Vulcan if you don't hit what you're aiming for. That being said it will probably be a hollowpoint of some kind. I know there is a preconception about hollowpoints being inhumane due to their prohibition in war by the Hague Convention. However on the other side bullets that don't expand or flatten are explicitly illegal for use in hunting due to them being less likely to quickly and humanely dispatch an animal. And hollowpoints are in extensive use with police forces around the world due to the fact that they are more likely to stop in a target, making them less likely to overpenetrate and cause unintentional harm to bystanders or damage to the environment. I'm not going to have much in the way of specific suggestions for ammunition, as this is going to be contingent on the specific firearm you choose. Ammunition can be designed for shorter barreled compact guns or the longer barreled full sized guns. You can find ammunition designed for lower recoil if you can better and more reliably hit your target with it. A hollowpoint with a wider mouth can be more conducive to expanding, but a wider hollowpoint may not function as well in certain firearms as compared to others. If you are still morally opposed to expanding bullets 9mm NATO loadings are designed to be more powerful than standard ammunition to help them deliver more energy to help provide that stop. If your local gun shop has an online store start there and see what they have in stock for defensive ammo in your caliber. The best ammo in the world has no value if you can't get it. See what people are saying about those online. Try to take a look at what people are using for your gun or for guns like it. And definitely test the ammo out in your own gun. It's ok to practice and train with cheaper range ammo, but do try to do some training with the ammo you are planning on using so you are familiar with the way it runs in your gun, the firing impulse, and the point of impact it has. As different ammunition loadings can have an impact on where your gun is shooting. Don't forget to rotate your ammo. Fluctuations in temperature can have a negative impact on the propellants in ammunition. If you've been carrying the same rounds for a long time fire them and replace them with a new batch. It's unlikely for them to degrade enough to be an issue unless you go a very prolonged time without changing them out. But it is a good practice to keep for consistency.
A minor aside about lead. Lead is used in most projectiles but this isn't the only source of lead contamination from firearms. The priming compound most commonly used to ignite a round is lead styphnate. Lead styphnate decomposes into lead oxide which is one of the lead compounds more likely to leave lead in your body after exposure. Avoid touching any of your mucous membranes after handling firearms, i.e. your eyes or your mouth. Any soap should be good enough to clean the lead off of your hands, but if you are particularly worried they do make soap specifically designed to remove lead, or even just something more concentrated like dishsoap can help. Make sure you do a proper 30+ second lather and get under your nails. Cold water can help to keep your pores closed.
Buying a set of A-Zoom snap caps in your caliber can be helpful as they will let you practice loading and unloading a firearm without using live ammunition. And will cushion the firing pin keeping it from unintentional damage while doing dry fire. Dry fire meaning triggering the gun without having ammunition in it. Which is an important part of training and maintenance. It can be tempting to buy Tipton snap caps, but avoid those they are not good.
How do I clean guns? There's a couple of things I'd recommend you have when starting out. The first is a rod with an appropriately sized bore brush. The inside of the barrel is the only thing that really requires a special tool to clean, that being a rod like the one pictured above. For the rest q-tips, paper towel, and cleaning rags/cloths are fine. I find microfiber cloths get dirty very quickly so I tend to avoid them. For wet ingredients I recommend any solvent, CLP (clean, lube, protect,) and an oil. These can be found, generally speaking, in any store that sells firearms or firearm accessories. The solvent I personally use is G96 Nitro Solvent. Use this to break up any carbon fouling on the outside of the gun or the inside of the bore. Anhydrous rubbing alcohol can be used in place of a solvent. The CLP I use is G96 Gun Treatment. When I am doing lighter cleaning and don't need the more intensive solvent I use this to break up carbon and leave a protective film over the metal parts of my guns. For an oil I use Hoppes No.9 Lubricating Oil. A good rule of thumb is to lightly oil any bearing surfaces between moving parts. But any low viscosity oil will work. I know people that just use a low viscosity automotive oil, it works just fine and the bottles last almost forever. In a pinch you can use hot soapy water to clean guns. Just bear in mind any steel part that is left wet can lead to rusting. As well as depending on the soap you use you can have a residue build up on your gun. Anhydrous rubbing alcohol can be used to help dry out parts after you've cleaned with water. Steel/polymer picks, nylon/brass/steel brushes, and punch sets can also be helpful to have around when doing gun maintenance. "Bore Snakes" basically a rope with copper bristles can be convenient for cleaning the inside of a barrel, but won't help you with clearing a stuck casing like a rod will.
I won't go to deep into storage. Pick something that suits your needs and fits your environment. I will mention the Lock Picking Lawyer on YouTube has done quick access handgun safes in the past and works as review of those. Some of them are remarkably poor in design. And as a word of warning anything can be broken into. I once had to look up proof of sale for a gentleman who had his safe stolen from him. It was an entire standing gun safe that he had set into his wall and drilled into the studs. The people that robbed him cut it straight out of the studs in his wall. Be mindful of the people to whom you mention your firearm ownership.
And now a word of warning from me. On April 18th and 19th, 2020, a man in Nova Scotia committed one of the worst mass killings in Canadian history. The man did this in part with three firearms he obtained from an American gun owner. The murderer was doing handiwork for the American and requested a handgun in lieu of payment, he received that in what was an illegal straw purchase, and stole 2 others from the American. 22 people were killed and 3 were injured. Be mindful of the fact that your actions have consequences. A momentary lapse can make for major consequences. It doesn't necessarily have to be a mass shooting, 900 children under the age of 5 have found a firearm and shot themselves or another between the years of 2015 and 2022. All preventable. I like firearms, but I know what they do and I know what they are capable of in the wrong hands. For a lot of people a firearm is a solid piece of certainty that they can hold in their hands in an increasingly uncertain world. But be aware of the burden that accompanies them. They are all lethal weapons and demand the care and consideration of a lethal weapon. Try to take care of yourself and the people around you. With that being said I will end the post here. If anybody has any questions I will do my best to answer here or in my dms. If you'd like to admonish me for being a merchant of death you are free to do so, know that I probably won't engage though. If I have made any mistakes here, or if you have other considerations that I have not touched on please do leave them in the comments. I am only one man and there is only so much I can do to ensure my own accuracy. Be well. I hope you never need this.
item.dc:creator: Baelnorn
